coredata.dc:title: Long-wavelength emission room-temperature phosphorescent carbon dots activated by an ortho-carboxyl substitution strategy and employed for achieving tunable LED
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Zihao
Feng, Zhiying
Zhou, Sen
Yang, Xiaoming
coredata.dc:description: While room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) associated with carbon dots (CDs) has been widely achieved, obtaining long-wavelength emission RTP, especially while mitigating the quenching effect of water or dissolved oxygen, remains a challenging yet desirable goal. We synthesized three types of phosphorescent CDs with varying luminescent properties by using three precursors with carboxyls positioned differently. The formation process of these CDs resulted in a well-ordered and compact structure, effectively inhibiting molecular vibrations and reducing non-radiative transitions. Consequently, it successfully prevented the quenching effect of dissolved oxygen on the RTP of CDs in an aqueous environment. Significantly, PA-AIBN synthesized with phthalic acid, rather than iso-phthalic acid and terephthalic acid, exhibited long-wavelength emission RTP, with the phosphorescent emission center reaching as far as 640 nm. To be specific, the ortho-substituted carboxyls played a critical role in boosting the formation of intramolecular hydrogen bonds. Simultaneously, an increase in the doping levels of both nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) in PA-AIBN facilitated the long-wavelength emission RTP. The increased sp2 conjugated carbon core of PA-AIBN narrowed the optical band gap, contributing to the long-wavelength emission. Taken together, these factors cooperatively promoted the long-wavelength emission of RTP. Importantly, the PA-AIBN synthesized in this study exhibited a broad half-peak width and long-wavelength emission. It was successfully used in the preparation of white-light-emitting diodes without the need for commercial phosphor powder, demonstrating its enormous potential for practical lighting devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Long-wavelength emission
Room-temperature phosphorescence
Ortho-substituted carboxyl
White light emitting diode
Tunable correlated color temperature

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of PA-AIBN, IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN
Synthesis and characterization of PA-AIBN, IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN
Structural and luminescent exploration of PA-AIBN, IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN
Elucidating the mechanism of regulating their fluorescence and phosphorescence
Constructing the structural models of PA-AIBN, IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN
Preparation of white-light emitting diodes (WLEDs) using PA-AIBN
Applications of PA-AIBN, IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN as the fluorescent ink in information encryption

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of PA-AIBN (A), IPA-AIBN(B) and TPA-AIBN (C); (D) Fluorescent CIE coordinates of PA-AIBN, IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN; Phosphorescence maximum excitation and emission spectra of PA-AIBN (E), IPA-AIBN (F) and TPA-AIBN (G); (H) Phosphorescent CIE coordinates of PA-AIBN, IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN.

Phosphorescence lifetime decay of PA-AIBN (A), IPA-AIBN (B) and (C) TPA-AIBN; (D) UV–vis spectra of PA-AIBN, IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN; (E) Images of PA-AIBN, IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN under the daylight, 365 nm of UV and UV off; Phosphorescence spectra of PA-AIBN (F), IPA-AIBN (G) and TPA-AIBN (H) with varying temperature.

HR-TEM images of PA-AIBN (A), IPA-AIBN (B) and TPA-AIBN (C); FTIR spectra (D), XPS (full survey) (E) and XRD patterns (F) of PA-AIBN, IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN; TG-DSC curves of (G) PA-AIBN, (H) IPA-AIBN and (I) TPA-AIBN.

Images of PA-AIBN (A), IPA-AIBN (B) and TPA-AIBN (C) in water under daylight, 365 nm of UV and UV off; Energy level diagram of PA-AIBN (D), IPA-AIBN (E), TPA-AIBN (F); Diffuse reflectance spectra of (G) PA-AIBN, (H) IPA-AIBN and (I) TPA-AIBN; (J) Optical band gaps of PA-AIBN, IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN.

Structural models of PA-AIBN (A), IPA-AIBN (B) and TPA-AIBN (C) as well as their corresponding two-dimensional structures; (D) Aspects of regulating their luminescent red-shift for PA-AIBN, IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN; (E) Elaborating the resistance mechanism of PA-AIBN towards water. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Emission spectra and CIE coordinates of blank InGaN chip (A, B), warm-white LED (D, E), pure-white LED (G, H) and cold-white LED (J, K) prepared by PA-AIBN, insets: Photographs of WLEDs under daylight (a) and being working (b); Photographs of Bing Dwen Dwen (c) and dolls (d) with illumination of blank InGaN chip (C) and warm-white LED (F); Photographs of doll (c) and magazine (d) with illumination of pure-white LED (I) and cold-white LED (L).
Illustration of synthesizing PA-AIBN and its corresponding applications.

head.ce:title: Long-wavelength emission room-temperature phosphorescent carbon dots activated by an ortho-carboxyl substitution strategy and employed for achieving tunable LED

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Academy of Metrology and Quality Inspection, Chongqing 401121, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Academy of Metrology and Quality Inspection

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chongqing Academy of Metrology and Quality Inspection, Chongqing 401121, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Zihao Yan and Zhiying Feng contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: While room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) associated with carbon dots (CDs) has been widely achieved, obtaining long-wavelength emission RTP, especially while mitigating the quenching effect of water or dissolved oxygen, remains a challenging yet desirable goal. We synthesized three types of phosphorescent CDs with varying luminescent properties by using three precursors with carboxyls positioned differently. The formation process of these CDs resulted in a well-ordered and compact structure, effectively inhibiting molecular vibrations and reducing non-radiative transitions. Consequently, it successfully prevented the quenching effect of dissolved oxygen on the RTP of CDs in an aqueous environment. Significantly, PA-AIBN synthesized with phthalic acid, rather than iso-phthalic acid and terephthalic acid, exhibited long-wavelength emission RTP, with the phosphorescent emission center reaching as far as 640 nm. To be specific, the ortho-substituted carboxyls played a critical role in boosting the formation of intramolecular hydrogen bonds. Simultaneously, an increase in the doping levels of both nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) in PA-AIBN facilitated the long-wavelength emission RTP. The increased sp2 conjugated carbon core of PA-AIBN narrowed the optical band gap, contributing to the long-wavelength emission. Taken together, these factors cooperatively promoted the long-wavelength emission of RTP. Importantly, the PA-AIBN synthesized in this study exhibited a broad half-peak width and long-wavelength emission. It was successfully used in the preparation of white-light-emitting diodes without the need for commercial phosphor powder, demonstrating its enormous potential for practical lighting devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials, traditionally encompassing pure organic, organic–inorganic hybrid, and polymer materials, have garnered significant attention owing to their controllable molecular structure, tunable photoluminescence, and extended lifetime. These characteristics have facilitated diverse applications of RTP materials in various fields, such as biochemical detection [1,2], information encryption [3,4], biological imaging [5,6], emergency lighting [7], and beyond. Nevertheless, acquiring traditional organic phosphorescent materials is challenging due to inefficient intersystem crossing (ISC) and rapid non-radiative decay [8]. Moreover, the triplet excitons of non-metallic phosphorescent materials are susceptible to quenching by dissolved oxygen or heating, hence their phosphorescent emissions can only be observed in their crystalline or composite states, severely restricting their applications [9,10]. Consequently, the development of a new generation of RTP materials with extended lifetimes, robust water tolerance, and low toxicity is both valuable and challenging.

Carbon dots (CDs), belonging to a distinctive class of carbon-based luminescent materials alongside fullerene, graphene and carbon nanotubes, have found extensive applications in various areas due to their excellent photostability, tunable photoluminescence, low toxicity and economic superiority [11–13]. In recent years, CDs exhibiting more exciting optical phenomena, such as RTP, thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF), electrochemical luminescence, mechanical luminescence, or near-infrared luminescence (NIR), have been extensively explored [14–16]. RTP CDs, generating stable phosphorescence without the introduction of metals or heavy atoms, stand out among other RTP materials, and researchers have raised kinds of methods towards their preparation. Specifically, RTP CDs are typically obtained through two approaches: suppressing the vibration and rotation of chromophores or enhancing spin–orbit coupling (SOC) to achieve effective ISC. Currently, a commonly used strategy for synthesizing RTP CDs is to provide a rigid environment through crystallization [17], polymerization [18] or introducing matrix such as PVA or PMMA [19,20]. This way effectively suppresses the vibration and rotation of CDs, thereby inhibiting non-radiative transitions and achieving RTP [17,21]. Another approach involves introducing heteroatoms (B, N, P, and S) or aromatic carbonyl groups, thus enhancing SOC. The abundant n-electrons of heteroatoms can improve the efficiency of ISC, resulting in long-lived RTP [22–24]. While these strategies are effective in obtaining RTP CDs, it remains challengeable to obtain the long-wavelength RTP CDs with self-protective properties [25,26].

Approaches to achieving long-wavelength phosphorescence include incorporating rare-earth metal centers or introducing aromatic carbonyls or heavy atoms into organometallic compounds. However, these methods suffer from drawbacks such as insufficient stability and high production cost [27]. Additionally, constructing composites using CDs and a matrix can narrow down the energy gap between the ground state and excited triplet state through energy transfer (ET), which is also considered as an effective approach to activate long-wavelength RTP [28,29]. For instance, a molecular sieve strategy was reported, producing red RTP by forming a composite with zeolite as the matrix [30]. However, the methods described above for obtaining long-wavelength phosphorescence emission are often constrained by the relatively complex preparation process and lower luminescent intensity. Additionally, introducing a matrix is also necessary to achieve stable room temperature phosphorescence, which is inefficient. Consequently, exploring simple strategies to generate the stable long-wavelength RTP CDs with self-protective property remains challenging yet significant.

In this study, we propose the synthesis of three original CDs with distinct luminescent properties using a one-step hydrothermal method. By regulating the positions of aromatic carboxyl groups in three phthalic acid (PA) precursors and introducing nitrogen and phosphorus elements at the presence of azodiisobutyronitrile (AIBN) and phosphoric acid, we successfully synthesized long-wavelength carbon polymer dots (Scheme 1 ) [31]. Notably, the ortho-carboxyl of PA played a key role in producing long-wavelength RTP through the spatial effect, which represented an innovative strategy for generating long-wavelength emission phosphorescence. Furthermore, the orderly formation of intermolecular forces during the reaction process of the CDs inhibited non-radiative transitions, effectively preventing RTP quenching by water or dissolved oxygen [32]. Importantly, the prepared CDs (PA-AIBN) exhibited a broader half-peak width, long-wavelength emission, and demonstrated superior characteristics for applications such as the preparation of white-light-emitting diode (WLED) and information encryption.

Phthalic acid (PA), isophthalic acid (IPA) and terephthalic acid (TPA) were procured from Yien Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 2, 2-azobisisobutyronitrile (AIBN) was sourced from Titan Scientific Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Phosphoric acid was acquired from Aladdin reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium hydroxide was acquired from Chron Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). The ultraviolet curing adhesive (organic silica gel) and InGaN chips were sourced from Zhanwang Long Technology Co., Ltd. (Shenzhen, China). Additionally, all commercial reagents were used directly without further purification, and ultrapure water produced by an Aquapro AWL-0502-P water system (18.25 MΩ·cm) was employed throughout the entire experimental procedure.

The synthesis of CDs commenced in a ZSX 1200 Muffle Furnace (Beijing, China). Fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra were recorded using a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan), and UV–Vis absorptions were recorded with a Shimadzu UV-3600i Plus ultraviolet visible spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). The morphology of CDs was characterized using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) with a TECNAI G2 F20 microscope (Columbia, America) operating at 200 kV. Additionally, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey of CDs was conducted using a K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (New York, America), and X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were acquired using the SmartLab X-ray diffraction analyzer. The surface groups of CDs were examined using a Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan). Phosphorescence spectra, quantum yields and lifetime measurements at different temperatures (ranging from 77 to 400 K) were performed using an Edinburgh FLS1000 photoluminescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh, Scotland) equipped with a microsecond flash-lamp (lF900, 100–0.1 Hz) and a time-gating device (delay time ranging from 0.001 to 9999 ms). For thermogravimetric analysis, a TG-DSC synchronous thermal analyzer was utilized. The spectra of the LED were recorded using a HAAS-2000 high accuracy array spectroradiometer, and the power for the LED was provided by a tunable direct-current (DC) voltage-stabilized source (MT-152D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PA-AIBN, IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare three CDs using hydrothermal method, 1.5 g of phthalic acid and 0.5 g of 2,2-azobisisobutyronitrile were initially mixed with 5 mL of purified water, followed by the slow addition of 1 mL of phosphoric acid. This resulting mixture was transferred into a para-polyphenyl reactor and sealed in the reaction kettle, which was then placed to the muffle furnace at 270 °C for 5 h. After cooling, the reaction kettle was opened to obtain the solid–liquid mixture, with the solution appearing as clear light-yellow. The brown-yellow solid, firmly adhered to the inner walls and bottom of the kettle, was carefully scraped off and rinsed with purified water three times. After drying, the brown-yellow powder was obtained and stored at room temperature with proper sealing, and it was named PA-AIBN throughout the entire experiment. Similarly, the synthesis processes of CDs using iso-phthalic acid or terephthalic acid as the carbon source were same to those with phthalic acid. The only difference was that all these CDs appeared as solid–liquid mixtures consisting of pure-white solid and colorless clarified solutions. After the separation and drying procedures, we named the final powders of these two CDs as IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN, respectively.

The ultraviolet curing adhesive and the powder of PA-AIBN were uniformly mixed at a room temperature with a mass ratio of 1:1, resulting in a light-brown transparent viscous solution, which served as the blue-light color conversion layer. Subsequently, the viscous solutions with different volume were separately and carefully dropped onto InGaN chips of 455 nm, thus forming the transparent droplets. These prepared chips were then transferred into an oven of 80 °C followed by baking for 1 h. After covering them with transparent lampshades, the white LED lamps were successfully assembled. Additionally, control experiments were designed and performed using IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN under the same conditions, and the two prepared LED lamps only emitted blue light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of PA-AIBN, IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN
ce:section.ce:para: The long-lived phosphor emissions originating from triplet excitons present an excellent prospect. However, achieving both multi-color and long-wavelength phosphorescence simultaneously is undoubtedly challenging due to the forbidden transition property governed by the Pauli Exclusion Principle. In our synthesis, AIBN played a dual role as the initiator of the polymerization reaction and as a dopant of N, while phosphoric acid served as a dopant of P to prepare the phosphorescent materials. The CDs (PA-AIBN) were synthesized using the one-step solvothermal method under optimized reaction conditions of 270 °C for 5 h, and the entire synthesis process was simultaneously characterized (Figs. S1 and S2). Reaction time and temperature usually show the direct impact on the carbonization and aggregation degree of CDs, thus regulating their optical properties. Increasing reaction temperature facilitated the formation of intramolecular hydrogen bonds, which promoted to form the more compact structures of CDs, effectively stabilizing triplet excitons and achieving the noticeable RTP [33].

Employing the optimized synthesis conditions, we successfully prepared three CDs with distinct luminescence properties. Specifically, PA derived PA-AIBN emitted orange-red phosphorescence, while both IPA derived IPA-AIBN and TPA derived TPA-AIBN displayed green phosphorescence. The fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra of these three CDs were recorded, and the powders of PA-AIBN, IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN exhibited strong fluorescence and RTP emissions (Fig. 1 ). Their maximal fluorescent emissions were measured at 572 nm, 383 nm and 406 nm, respectively, while the maximal phosphorescent emissions were found at 588 nm, 503 nm and 519 nm, respectively. Detailed fluorescence and phosphorescence CIE coordinates for the three CDs were calculated (Table S1). Notably, when compared with the fluorescence and phosphorescence of CDs derived from IPA and TPA, those derived from PA exhibited a significant red shift. This pronounced shift was also clearly showed in the CIE coordinate images of fluorescence (Fig. 1D) and phosphorescence (Fig. 1H). Furthermore, the quantum yields of PA-AIBN, IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN were explored with their optimal excitations. Their fluorescence quantum yields were respectively measured as 12.9 %, 20.2 % and 15.7 %, while their phosphorescence quantum yields were 5.4 %, 7.5 % and 5.8 %, respectively.

To confirm that the three CDs synthesized in this study emitted RTP rather than TADF, we measured their phosphorescence lifetimes at 298 K (Fig. 2 A, B and C), and obtained decay curves by conducting three-exponential fittings. The average phosphorescence lifetimes of PA-AIBN, IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN were determined to be 0.15 s, 0.96 s and 0.59 s, respectively (Table S2). In contrast to their fluorescence emissions (Fig. 1B and C), the phosphorescence of IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN (Fig. 1F and G) exhibited large Stokes shifts, leading us to preliminarily consider their afterglow emissions as RTP. Moreover, the afterglow lifetimes of IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN, 0.96 s and 0.59 s respectively, were much longer than the typical microseconds-long lifetime of TADF. This observation strongly supported that their afterglows were indeed RTP rather than TADF. For PA-AIBN, since its fluorescence and afterglow wavelengths were similar, we further measured its afterglow lifetime at 77 K and found it to be 0.23 s (Table S3) longer than that at 298 K (Fig. S3A). This result demonstrated that the afterglow of PA-AIBN was RTP rather than TADF.

To further investigate whether the afterglows of the three CDs were due to RTP or TADF, we performed phosphorescence spectra measurements at various temperatures, ranging from 77 K to 400 K, using optimal excitations for PA-AIBN, IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN (Fig. 2F, G and H). Our findings revealed that the afterglow intensity of all three CDs gradually decreased as the temperature increased. This observation strongly indicates that the afterglows exhibited by these CDs were indeed RTP and not TADF. These results are consistent with our previous lifetime measurements, further supporting that the afterglows of PA-AIBN, IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN were the results of RTP [34].

As it is universally acknowledged, the τ components of different lifetimes are associated with different types of electron transitions, corresponding to various luminescent centers [35]. For the RTP emission of PA-AIBN, we observed a major peak at 588 nm and two shoulder peaks at 543 nm and 640 nm (Fig. 1E). We also conducted the time-resolved phosphorescence decay measurements (Fig. S3B). The shoulder peak at 640 nm disappeared earlier, responsible for the orange-red phosphorescence. In contrast, the major peak at 588 nm lasted longer, associated with the orange-yellow phosphorescence. Consequenlty, the phosphorescence of the powder underwent a gradual change from instant orange-red to orange-yellow upon turning off the 365 nm of UV excitation (Fig. 2E). These findings demonstrated that the prepared orange-red PA-AIBN exhibited multiple emission centers [36].

To gain further insights into the types of electron transitions responsible for their luminous centers, we conducted UV–vis absorption spectra measurements for the three CDs (Fig. 2D). Specifically, the absorption bands at 250 nm and 310 nm were attributed to the electron transitions of π-π* and n-π* for the sp2 carbon core domain [22]. The UV absorptions of both IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN showed tails that extended to 400 nm. In contrast, PA-AIBN exhibited a broad absorption band at 390 nm, which was related to the surface states and displayed a noticeable overlap with its RTP excitation peak [37]. Moreover, this band extended up to 580 nm, indicating the presence of an extended conjugate of the carbon core, which is linked to the long-wavelength emission phosphorescence of PA-AIBN [38,39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and luminescent exploration of PA-AIBN, IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the three CDs was initially observed using HR-TEM, followed by obtaining size-distribution maps. The images revealed that all three CDs existed as spherical particles and were well-dispersed. The average diameters of PA-AIBN, IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN were measured to be 3.35 nm, 3.00 nm and 4.22 nm, respectively (Fig. 3 A, B and C). No obvious lattice structure was observed for these CDs, consistent with the characteristic of previously reported carbon polymer dots [36,40]. Next, we analyzed the functional groups of the three CDs using FTIR spectra (Fig. 3D). Normally, the vibration of C = O for free carboxylic acid occurs at 1760 cm−1, but for the three CDs, the vibrations of C = O emerged around 1690 cm−1. This shift was attributed to the carboxylic acid existing as a dimer through hydrogen bonding, causing the vibration to shift to a lower frequency. Additionally, the formation of hydrogen bonds shifted the stretching vibration of O-H to the range of 2900–3200 cm−1. It could also lead to average density of electron clouds as well a reduced molecular energy, further facilitating the shift of vibration frequency to a lower wavenumber [41]. In accordance with this theory, the O-H stretching vibration of PA-AIBN shifted to the lower wavenumber of 3087 cm−1, while IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN shifted to 3103 cm−1 and 3115 cm−1, respectively. These results demonstrated that PA-AIBN exhibited stronger interactions of hydrogen bonds than IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN. The carboxyl groups of PA were closely adjacent to each other, promoting the formation of intramolecular hydrogen bonds. These intramolecular hydrogen bonds could flatten the hydrogen bond sites to reduce steric hindrance while the intermolecular hydrogen bonds function as the direction connectors between molecules, resulting in the formation of hydrogen bond networks. Thus, PA-AIBN tended to form more regular and orderly structure [42]. In contrast, both IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN showed obvious absorption at around 3580 cm−1, derived from the stretching vibration of free O-H, indicating that fewer hydrogen bonds were formed for IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN. For PA-AIBN, the absence of an absorption at 3580 cm−1 suggested that most free-state O-H groups of PA-AIBN were involved in the process of forming hydrogen bonds. Altogether, the ortho-carboxyl groups of PA played a crucial role in promoting the formation of intramolecular hydrogen bonds, which reduced the steric hindrance and coupled with intermolecular hydrogen bonds, facilitating the formation of hydrogen bond networks for PA-AIBN.

To determine their elemental composition, XPS was employed, and four major characteristic peaks appeared at 284.1, 402.1, 532.1, and 134.1 eV for PA-AIBN, IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN, indicating that they mainly consisted of C 1 s, N 1 s, O 1 s and P 2p (Fig. 3E). According to the El-Sayed rule, the introduction of heteroatoms with lone pair electrons could facilitate the molecule’s transition from 1(n-π*) to 3(n-π*), thus improving the ISC efficiency and increasing the number of triplet excitons [29]. The higher doping amounts of N and P for PA-AIBN compared to IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN (Table S4) are closely related to their luminescence properties. In particular, the high-resolution C 1 s spectra of three CDs showed three peaks (Fig. S4A, E and I). The peak at 284.3 eV was attributed to C-C/C = C, with corresponding contents in the whole C 1 s spectrum being 86.15 %, 68.23 % and 65.51 % for PA-AIBN, IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN, respectively. This result further indicated the increased sp2 carbon core in PA-AIBN, leading to its extended conjugation and long-wavelength emission phosphorescence, consisting with the UV–vis data [39,43]. Additionally, peaks at 285.9 eV and 288.7 eV were attributed to C-O/C-N and C = O/C = N, respectively. Next, the high-resolution N 1 s spectra were divided into pyrrole nitrogen (400.70 eV) and graphite nitrogen (401.7 eV), with the amount of graphite nitrogen being higher in PA-AIBN compared to IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN (Fig. S4B, F and J). The O 1 s spectra were fitted with two peaks at 531.4 eV (C = O) and 532.7 eV (C-O/P-O) (Fig. S4C, G and K). For the P 2p spectra, two peaks at 133.7 eV (P-O) and 134.4 eV (P-N) were observed (Fig. S4D, H and L). These findings demonstrated the presence of C = C, C = O and C = N in the three CDs.

Furthermore, we obtained X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the three CDs (Fig. 3F) and the diffraction peaks were sharp, indicating a small full width at half maxima (FWHM). This observation confirmed the satisfactory powder crystallinity of the three CDs, which in turn suggested a rigid structure that inhibited molecular rotation and stabilized the excitations of the triplet states. Due to the incomplete carbonization of CDs, the surface became more disordered, and XRD signals from amorphous polymer chains on the surface, relatively strong, covered the signals from the internal graphitized region, thus there existed a significant overlap between the XRD patterns of the CDs and the raw materials (Fig. S5) [18,44]. Moreover, the results of thermogravimetric analysis and differential scanning calorimetry (TG-DSC) demonstrated the high thermal stability of the three CDs (Fig. 3G, H and I). Notably, the DSC curve of PA-AIBN showed two endothermic transitions. The peak at 205 °C was attributed to the release of water molecules from PA-AIBN, while another peak at 472 °C was associated with the fracture of the polymer chains, requiring a much higher temperature compared to IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN. These findings indicated the presence of more polymer chains on the surface of PA-AIBN.

We also obtained the specific cathodoluminescence (CL) spectra at 5 keV of three CDs, and each type exhibited its distinct peaks. The diverse cathodoluminescent emissions of three CDs synthesized using different precursors revealed their varied structures (Fig. S6A, B and C). Simultaneously, the peak shapes of three CDs basically showed similar with an increasing cycle of irradiation, suggesting their stable surface structures of three CDs (Fig. S6D, E and F). In contrast, there appeared the decreased CL fluorescence intensity of three CDs with experiencing multiple irradiations, which was mainly attributed to the localized damage caused by the electron irradiation or surface defects.

To sum up, synthesizing PA-AIBN using phthalic acid resulted in CDs with a higher degree of sp2 carbon core. Furthermore, three CDs with distinct phosphorescence properties were obtained by selecting precursors with aromatic carboxyls in different positions. Specifically, among them, the ortho-substituted carboxyls exhibited a superior hydrogen-bonding effect, effectively activating solid-state fluorescence and phosphorescence with long-wavelength emissions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Elucidating the mechanism of regulating their fluorescence and phosphorescence
ce:section.ce:para: Remarkably, the phosphorescence of all three CDs could still be observed when they were placed in aqueous environment, indicating that their phosphorescence was not quenched by water or dissolved oxygen (Fig. 4 A, B and C). This preservation of phosphorescence could be attributed to the rigid environment created by the formation of hydrogen bonds during the crystallization process.

In the context of the phosphorescence mechanism, it is crucial to enhance spin–orbit coupling to promote the ISC process, while simultaneously suppressing vibrational dissipation to stabilize the excited triplet state, thereby achieving long-lived RTP. In this study, three typical aromatic diacids, namely PA, IPA and TPA, were utilized. Their aromatic carbonyl groups play a crucial role in dramatically enhancing spin-orbital coupling, facilitating ISC and leads to the emission of long-lived RTP [45]. Another approach to achieving long-lived RTP is by reducing the energy gap (ΔEST) between the lowest excited singlet state (S1) and the lowest excited triplet state (T1). To analyze this, the ΔEST of the three CDs was calculated based on the low-temperature fluorescence emission spectra (Fig. S7A, B and C) and phosphorescence emission spectra at 77 K, considering the reduced vibrational dissipation at low temperatures. The results showed that the singlet–triplet energy gaps of PA-AIBN, IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN were 0.045 eV, 0.712 eV and 0.552 eV, respectively (Fig. 4D, E and F). All three CDs exhibited small energy gaps from S1 to T1, providing an easier intersystem crossing process. Consequently, a higher number of triplet excitons were generated for all three CDs, leading to the manifestation of long-lived RTP.

To further understand why PA-AIBN exhibited longer-wavelength emissions of fluorescence and phosphorescence compared to IPA-AIBN or TPA-AIBN, we recorded their UV diffuse reflectance spectra. By analyzing the Tauc plot, we calculated the optical band gap (Eg) between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of PA-AIBN, IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN as 2.523, 3.507 and 3.459 eV, respectively (Fig. 4G, H and I), revealing the narrowest band gap of PA-AIBN (Fig. 4J). According to Hückel theory, a narrower band gap is associated with more atomic orbitals contributing to the conjugate π bonds [46]. Thereby, the narrowed band gap of PA-AIBN was responsible for the red-shift in its solid-state fluorescence and phosphorescence emission, aligning with the increased degree of sp2 carbon core in PA-AIBN.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Constructing the structural models of PA-AIBN, IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN
ce:section.ce:para: To further elaborate and substantiate the propensity of PA-AIBN to form a denser hydrogen bond network during the synthesis, we employed the software of Gaussian 16 to further simulate and elucidate the structures of three CDs. Utilizing the xTB method, we performed the conformational searches and structural optimizations for three CDs with the same precursor proportions. As shown in Fig. 5 A, B and C, the amount of hydrogen bonds formed in PA-AIBN was notably higher than that of IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN, consistent with the previously proposed theory. These models were constructed with relatively smaller molecular weights, only presenting partial structures of the three CDs. These findings indicated the formation of hydrogen bond networks, which could enhance their stability in the triplet state. The denser hydrogen bond network in PA-AIBN may contribute to the redshift in the emission wavelength.

In summary, the three types of CDs all formed effective hydrogen bond networks during the synthesis process, enabling them to resist the quenching effects of water molecules and dissolved oxygen on the triplet excitons in water (Fig. 5E). Simultaneously, CDs synthesized from precursors with carboxyl substitutions at different positions exhibited distinct optical properties. As depicted in Fig. 5D, there is a noticeable trend from TPA-AIBN and IPA-AIBN to PA-AIBN, showing an observable increase in sp2 carbon core size, more heteroatom doping, higher graphitic nitrogen content, and larger hydrogen bond network. These factors collectively resulted in a narrower bandgap and a redshift in wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of white-light emitting diodes (WLEDs) using PA-AIBN
ce:section.ce:para: Currently, solid-state light-emitting CDs show great promise as potential replacements for traditional phosphors LED preparation. However, to achieve ideal white LED (WLED) materials, CDs with a larger FWHM emission are required. Otherwise, the addition of commercial phosphor powder becomes necessary to obtain white light. The emissions of the PA-AIBN CDs prepared in this study covered a wide range from 500 to 800 nm, demonstrating their long-wavelength solid-state emission. This led us to explore the possibility of directly employing PA-AIBN for economical WLEDs preparation, without the addition of commercial phosphor powder. Indeed, we successfully used PA-AIBN to prepare WLEDs and obtained its corresponding emission spectrum (Fig. 6 D). Notably, the calculated CIE coordinates of the WLED were (0.39, 0.40) with a correlated color temperature (CCT) of 3955 K, while its color rendering index (Ra) reached 72.8 (Fig. 6E), indicating its potential of serving as a warm-white LED. In contrast, the control LED using a 455 nm InGaN chip without any phosphor powder exhibited a blue LED (BLED) emission (Fig. 6A and B). Similarly, LEDs utilizing IPA-AIBN (Fig. S8A) and TPA-AIBN (Fig. S8B) as phosphors emitted blue light, confirming the advantage of the larger FWHM and long-wavelength emission in preparing WLED with PA-AIBN. Furthermore, we obtained the WLED with different CCT by regulating the mass of PA-AIBN. With the introduced amount of PA-AIBN decreasing, its emission peak exhibited a blue shift, and the corresponding intensity decreased while the CCT increased. To be specific, we obtained a pure-white LED with CIE coordinates of (0.34, 0.34), a color rendering index (Ra) of 77.4 (Fig. 6G) and a color temperature of 5312 K (Fig. 6H) through decreasing the amount of PA-AIBN. By further deceasing the addition of PA-AIBN, we acquired a cold-white LED with CIE coordinates of (0.31, 0.30), Ra of 80.9 (Fig. 6J) and a color temperature of 6633 K (Fig. 6K), which could meet the standard for indoor lighting. In addition, we investigated the fluorescence and phosphorescence stability of PA-AIBN under the constant excitation, and the results demonstrated that both fluorescence and phosphorescence intensity generally showed stable (Fig. S9A and B). Followed by heating at 90 °C for an hour, the fluorescence and phosphorescence intensity of PA-AIBN scarcely decreased (Fig. S9C and D), indicating its potential towards the prolonged illumination.

To assess the practicability of the prepared LEDs for actual lighting applications, we conducted a visual experiment using Bing Dwen Dwen (the mascot of the Beijing Olympic Winter Games in 2022) and two dolls placed on a table. The illumination was provided by both the BLED and the warm-white LED prepared using PA-AIBN, IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN as phosphors. Under the illumination of the PA-AIBN-derived warm-white LED, the appearance of Bing Dwen Dwen and the two dolls could be clearly observed (c and d in Fig. 6F). Nevertheless, when subjected to irradiation from BLEDs fabricated using IPA-AIBN (c and d in Fig. S8A) and TPA-AIBN (c and d in Fig. S8B), discerning their true characteristics proved to be challenging. Additionally, we illuminated the doll and magazine with pure-white LED (Fig. 6I) and cold-white LED (Fig. 6L) both prepared using PA-AIBN, and the two targets exhibited excellent color reproduction. These results suggested that the lighting performance of WLED using PA-AIBN outperformed that of IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN, meeting the standard requirements for daily indoor lighting. This finding indicated the great potential of using PA-AIBN to prepare commercial lighting devices that offer the superior illumination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of PA-AIBN, IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN as the fluorescent ink in information encryption
ce:section.ce:para: In the field of fluorescent ink and information encryption, we conducted a series of explorations using three synthesized CDs. Initially, as shown in Fig. S10A, we employed the three synthesized CDs to draw a set of patterns. Due to variations in the fluorescence color and intensity of the three CDs under a 365 nm UV lamp, as well as differences in phosphorescence color and afterglow duration, the colors of the patterns exhibited a time-dependent trend after turning off the UV lamp. Building on this, we conducted a straightforward encryption design using PA-AIBN and IPA-AIBN. As illustrated in Fig. S10B, the depicted pattern displayed the letter sequence “PQT” under UV light. After turning off the UV light, the color of the letter sequence changed over time, eventually revealing the encrypted message “DOT.” Furthermore, we utilized these two types of CDs to depict the logo of Southwest University, as shown in Fig. S10C. Finally, employing the three types of CDs, we devised a simplified Trifid Cipher, and the specific information of the codebook is presented in Table S5. With the synthesized CDs and the designed codebook, we could efficiently encrypt and decrypt information. We demonstrated this process using the letter sequence “CARBON” and the results were depicted in Fig. S10D. In this section, we showcased the potential applications of these three CDs in information encryption and as fluorescent inks, highlighting their significant practicality and value.

In summary, we successfully synthesized three types of phosphorescent CDs with solid-state fluorescence and RTP by using three precursors with carboxyl groups at different positions. Among them, PA-AIBN exhibited long-wavelength emission RTP. The rigid structures formed during crystallization stabilized the excited triplet states of all three CDs, enabling self-protective RTP. Significantly, we investigated and elucidated the factors behind the long-wavelength emission RTP of PA-AIBN. The long-wavelength emission of PA-AIBN compared to IPA-AIBN and TPA-AIBN was attributed to several factors: the formation of intramolecular hydrogen bonds due to the ortho-carboxyls of phthalic acid, increased doping of N and P, and the presence of a sp2 conjugated carbon core. The synergistic effects of these factors resulted in the long-wavelength emission of PA-AIBN. Consequently, we introduced a novel strategy for achieving long-wavelength emission RTP. Furthermore, we directly used PA-AIBN to explore its application in the field of information encryption and to prepare WLEDs without using any commercial phosphor powder. This innovative and economical approach provides a practical solution for household lighting, demonstrating the great potential of PA-AIBN for commercial LED applications.

Zihao Yan: Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zhiying Feng: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Sen Zhou: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Xiaoming Yang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by SAMR Science and Technology Program (2023MK102), Chongqing Scientific Institution Incentive Performance Guiding Special Projects (CSTB2023JXJL-YFX0027), Chongqing Municipal Market Supervision Administration Science and Technology Program (CQSJKJDW2023005) and Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (SWU-XDJH202321).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.152835.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
